frameworks.name: optimal encoding and decoding
frameworks.one_line: joint theory of efficient sensory encoding matched to a perceptual decoder

frameworks.name: psychophysical law
frameworks.one_line: lawful mathematical relation linking discriminability to perceptual bias across stimulus space

core_question: Is there a lawful mathematical relation between perceptual bias and discrimination threshold across stimulus value?
key_assumptions: sensory encoding and perceptual decoding share identical assumptions about environmental statistics
perception is characterized by discrimination threshold and average perceived bias
encoder and decoder are jointly optimized
manipulations: stimulus value as the independent variable
contextual modulation conditions across reported datasets
key_findings: perceptual bias and discriminability follow a simple mathematical relation as functions of stimulus value
the relation holds across a wide range of psychophysical datasets
the relation captures perceptual changes induced by contextual modulation
interpretations: the bias-discriminability link may represent a general psychophysical law
sensory encoding and perceptual decoding are matched and co-optimized on shared environmental priors
